Hello friends and welcome to Six Figure Emails installment number five. This one is affectionately called the DMV downsell. As always, this is how the session is going to go down. We're going to give a little bit of a background on the template, how it came to be, why we use it, why we use it, so what are the persuasion triggers we're really looking to leverage? What are we coaching when it comes to coaching the conversion? What is the context that a downsell offers us that is unique and different from the main launch we're going to explore? All of that in full detail then we're going to explain where to use it. Yes, this is a downsell and it kind of feels logical, but there is some strategy to bear in mind here. So we are going to look very specifically about where to send it, where not to send it, then of course how to use it, some questions to ask to extract the content, types of downsells. There are more than one, there are many types of downsells you can explore. Some might be more relevant and appropriate for your audience and others. So we will take a look at those, then we will take a look at the template itself. And then in the next video below this one we have two examples, both from Todd Herman's 90 Day Year and Dan Martel. So that's how things are going down, but first the background. Why is it called the DMV downsell? Well, this is one of my all-time favorite 10x emails moments. So in case you don't know, 10x emails is the signature email training at Copy Hackers. And when we first launched it a few years ago, we had a Facebook group where people could share how they're using it, ask questions and just really ask for guidance on any emails they were writing. And we had one student, the very amazing Tara Gentili, she just wrote that she finished her main launch, things went really, really well, and she was just debating and contemplating sending a downsell to the people who just weren't a right fit for her more higher ticket program at the time. And I got that message right away and I was in a coffee shop and all of a sudden I got really interested in seeing what was possible here. So he actually jumped on a call spontaneously and whipped up a downsell email based on the principles we're going to talk about right now and she actually wrote it while waiting in line at the DMV. So in about, I don't know, an hour, I guess it takes to wait in line. She wrote her whole downsell sequence and pulled in an extra five figures of revenue, I believe. So powerful stuff, downsells are awesome and using the principles we're going to share and talk about more today, they could be a really profitable part of your launch. So a well-written downsell that you can write in line at the DMV if you're so fortunate to find yourself there can be the single most profitable email of your launch and by far the most trackable. It not only gives you a second chance at a sale, but it gives your audience, your subscribers a second chance at success in a way that is more appropriate to them. And this is all about what J. Abraham talks about as preeminence, really having their best interests in mind, sometimes a downsell. It's not about adding cash to our launch numbers. Sometimes it's really just about giving your audience what they most needed at that time. From a persuasion standpoint, we're actually capitalizing on some highly active psychological triggers that are present immediately following the close of an ever launch or an evergreen campaign. We're going to look exactly what those are in just a bit. And of course, you can create long lasting goodwill to set up your next campaign. All right, all that said, I of course had my early struggles with downsells. They tended to all follow the same script essentially coming back the next day and guilting the crap out of your customer, guilting the crap out of your prospects. Sorry, making them feel guilty about not buying the main thing and saying, here's something else, maybe this is more interesting to you or it's cheaper or it's less valuable or whatever it is. But it always felt a bit too much of a guilt trip, kind of like your, yeah, really just kind of wrangling them and forcing them into something cheaper that may or may not actually be of service. So it never felt right, the ones that I would read, the ones that I used to write earlier in my career and really thought we could do it a better way. So here are some of the other big mistakes that I currently see that I've done myself and that really limit the impact and potential that a downsell can have. So the first big one is second class products for second class customers. Don't come out with the tone that the main thing that just ended is first class and now they are getting something worse. No one wants to feel like they're getting something of less value than the people who just acted. Instead, they want to feel empowered. They want to feel smart and validated for having waited. Like what they are going to get now was actually exactly what they needed and they were smart for waiting. That's the feeling we want to create instead. Painting the FOMO, the fear of missing out or scarcity on too thick. So yes, of course, your downsell is going to be deadline driven, typically on a much shorter launch window than your main launch and we'll talk about that a little later. But that said, if you're relying on the same scarcity countdown timers and fear of missing out as the first time, well, that's not really going to be your best bet because these are the same people who didn't respond to those exact same things just a day or two ago in the main campaign. So we don't want to double down on the exact same persuasion triggers that just didn't work. Just doesn't make sense. Other thing, making a compromise. You don't want to feel like the downsell that they're buying is a compromise. That they're meeting you halfway in any way. That's really what we mean by like the whole marketer is trying to guilt the prospects into the downsell. Guilt is really a terrible terrible energy to infuse into your sales messages and compromises not the feeling that we want people coming into our universe with. It just doesn't serve them and it doesn't serve us. And then of course not being transparent or using tricks. Your market is probably going to be suspicious of your intent. They're probably savvy enough to know that this downsell isn't necessarily based on these random survey results that you did or didn't get or that you're extending the main launch because some emails didn't go out. Whatever it is, just be transparent. Don't use these weird tactics. Just be totally honest that what you have here may be a better use and value for them. Cool. So this is one last quote on Stephen Covey about synergy and compromise. So he says synergy is not the same as compromise and a compromise one plus one equals one and a half at best. Everybody loses something and that's your typical downsell. The marketer or the company loses revenue and the customer loses value in the product. So that's compromise. We want to avoid that synergy on the other hand is not just resolving a conflict. When we get to synergy, we transcend the conflict. We go beyond it to something new, something that excites everyone with fresh promise and transforms the future. And that's what we want to tap into when crafting both our downsell offer and the copy that supports it. So all this got me thinking in the spirit of coaching the conversion, which is really the ethos behind all of this. How do we meet our prospect where they're at, you know, fresh off of not buying the main product and then give them something new, exciting, more empowering, more appropriate to step into? How do we leverage the post campaign context and its unique psychological triggers, which we'll talk about in just a second? And how do we do it all while building our offer suite, generating more revenue and keeping all customers happy? So big lofty task all for the downsell. But here's why we use it. One of the best times to make a sale is right after the main launch. Here is what your prospect is experiencing. They're experiencing not just a fear of missing out, but the feeling of missing out. One is much more powerful than the other, you know, as the final hours of your initial campaign or launch was dwindling down. That's the fear of missing out. Well, by the time the downsell comes, they missed it. They are left with the feeling, which is a lot more stronger and acute than simply the fear. There's also relational imbalance. So if you provided a ton of value in your pre-launch, whether through a free video series, your emails, a challenge, a webinar, whatever it was in your pre-launch or your launch sequence, you've given a ton of value. They haven't been able to reciprocate yet because the main offer wasn't a right fit for them. So right now there's a relational imbalance that needs to be resolved. It feels like there's a relationship that needs to be put back into homeostasis. And on your customer's end, there's this kind of more subtle need to get things back in balance. So it's subtle, but it exists. And having the right offer in the downsell will definitely cue that up and give them a chance to bring things back into balance. Underserved and unloved, this is a feeling they have after, right? They participated, they invested time, energy, and attention in your main launch. Maybe they spent over five hours consuming your videos, your content, your emails leading up to this. And now they're kind of left with nothing. So that feels a little bit like they're not getting a return on their energy investment. They're feeling underserved and unloved on the whole end of it. So this is an opportunity to resolve that and have them be implicit in resolving it with you. And then of course, there's regret and disempowered around not pulling the trigger and not being in a position to. So maybe they either regret it because they wanted to and they just couldn't do it. Or maybe they felt disempowered because they weren't a right fit. Either of those contexts just creates something that you don't want people on your list to feel after a launch, right? These aren't exactly the emotions that are going to lead to a purchase later. Instead, you want to come in with something that gives them a chance to resolve it almost immediately. So your job is to meet them on these grounds, understand this context that we just spoke about, and turn the feeling of missing out into a feeling of having held off for something better. This is really the crux of it all. You want to elicit the feeling, good thing I waited. You want to give them a better second chance to reciprocate for all the value you've given them leading up to this. You want to make them feel heard and served. You know, just acknowledging that this is probably what will serve you better at this time. Here it is. You know, that's what your down sell offer is. And the combination of all this leaves them feeling excited and re-empowered to take you up on that offer. So as a whole, this email is coaching release from that feeling of missing out almost immediately after the main launch. Confirmation of why they didn't buy. You know, decisions are more empowering than letting an opportunity slip. So you want to make it feel like they made a decision not to buy, not that they just let the countdown timer do its thing and they were indifferent on it. You want to confirm you decided not to buy this. Here's why you didn't do it and here's why it may have been a good thing. And you want to reinforce that previous decision, which will build that self trust trust in you consistency, which leads to a re-empowering them to choose a more appropriate fit, which is going to be your down sell offer and then a shift in self perception. You don't want them to perceive themselves as a non buyer. You want them to perceive themselves as a smart buyer. They decided not to buy the first offer. They knew it wouldn't be the right fit for them. But now they are more empowered to channel that smart buyer ship into your down sell. Cool. So where to use it? Obviously we want to schedule this within 48 hours of the main launch, ending any longer after that and you risk losing that fear, that feeling of missing out effect as well as the other persuasion triggers we mentioned earlier. You know, ideally I would send this a day later. Some marketers would prefer to leave a day break before getting into this, but really not more than 48 hours later or else you really do lose some of that effect. Also you want to have the launch window much shorter than your initial one. So two to three days, 48 to 72 hours I'd say is ideal. Otherwise you really run the risk of launch exhaustion, especially if the main launch was, you know, already seven days. Last thing you want to do is have half a month of being in launch mode. It just kind of brings, yeah, a little bit of exhaustion around launching and takes out some of the urgency and seriousness of when you do it later. So all that said, how to use it? Here are some questions that are going to help you with the content. So without looking at price, you know, if price wasn't an issue and what ways was your audience not ready or empowered to benefit from your initial offer? So price isn't the main objection. What is? How are they not empowered to get the value from it? And what ways was their decision not to buy a smart one? This is a big one you want to spend some time on. Looking at the downsell options that we're going to look at in a second, what might they be in a better position to receive value from? And what unique characteristics exclusively empower them to make the most of the new offer? Maybe they don't need $5,000 of a, you know, a group coaching program. Maybe they're so autonomous and self-driven and self-led that they could have more of a done-for-you version. So self-motivated autonomy, these are all positive traits that might actually empower them, exclusively empower them to take a cheaper, cheaper, more appropriate option. Maybe they're very cautious and, you know, the first one was a new product, right? And they didn't really want to be kind of a guinea pig on a new product. You can call out the fact that they were cautious on that and then offer them a product that's already been used by dozens or hundreds or thousands of people with proven case studies. So these are just a few things you might look at, but you do want to highlight at least one characteristic that really feels empowering and makes them empowered to take the new offer. So you want to tie the trait with the offer, with the new offer together. All right, so four types of downsells you could do. It could be a stripped-down product without the hand-holding. So it could be all the prerecorded elements of a course, for example, without any support calls, without any live events, stuff like that. So just stripped-down version of the main product. It could be a shorter subscription period or a trial. So that could be for software instead of selling an annual license. It could be a three-month license or even a 14-day trial. A more introductory product within your suite that empowers them for the full product at a later date, maybe the main offer was both more expensive and too advanced, but even if they did spend the money, they're just not at a stage in their journey where they can get the full value of the product. So that's another one you want to look at, and then a splintered part of the main product designed to give a crunchy valuable more immediate results. So this is very relevant for online courses. There might be a specific lesson or a specific model within the whole thing that can give them a crunchy valuable result. And gives you a chance to really showcase how valuable the full product is and have a chance to upsell them into the full thing later. So these are four types of down-sells. There's guaranteed to be at least one here that makes sense for your audience to give them all a thorough look, brainstorm it, and just see which one answers the question of why was it a good thing that they didn't buy the first one and what do they most need right now? And that's your question that will help you zero in on the right type of down-sell. All right, so template itself, remind them of the offer they missed. This is triggering that feeling of missing out. Show you care that they're still on your mind that you haven't just moved on to serve all these new customers in your universe, but you're still thinking about these people here who missed out and are perhaps feeling underserved. Confirm that they made the right decision. This is really all about building that trust, taking any guilt out of the equation and building that trust before the offer comes into play. Commend them on that decision and why it was the right one. Once again, not just saying you made the right decision, but explaining why. You know, this is the same thing we spoke about in market search for meaning. It's not enough to just say it wasn't your fault. You need to unpack that. Why wasn't it your fault in a emotionally compelling or intellectually gratifying way? So, commend them on the decision, then transition. What about their current stage or unique abilities makes them exclusively empowered for your down-sell offer? Introduce the offer, have a call to action, and just give an unselfish reason for it. Maybe you're not even making a massive profit on it. Maybe this really just is about giving them what they need to continue on their journey and feel served right now. And then, yes, at the very end, you can put the urgency, the scarcity, the guarantee. Maybe you're only keeping it open for 48 hours, whatever it is. Let them know and close with another call to action. So, that is it for the main template in the next video. Below this one, we actually have two examples from live launches so you can see how this played out. So, catch the next video and I will see you soon.
